findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a well-defined, lucent lesion located within an epiphyseal equivalent, such as the patella, greater trochanter, or greater tuberosity [1], [2]. The lesion typically demonstrates a thin sclerotic margin and a narrow zone of transition [1], [3].
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include the presence of internal chondroid matrix calcifications, which are seen in approximately 50% of cases [1], [2]. On MRI, the lesion classically exhibits significant surrounding bone marrow and soft tissue edema, which is out of proportion to the size of the lesion [4], [1], [2].
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of a permeative or moth-eaten pattern of bone destruction to suggest an aggressive malignancy. Furthermore, there is no non-sclerotic margin or absence of internal matrix to suggest a giant cell tumor [5], [6].
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the combination of a well-defined epiphyseal lucent lesion with a sclerotic margin and striking perilesional marrow edema in a skeletally immature patient [5], [7], [1].

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Chondroblastoma, given the epiphyseal equivalent location, narrow zone of transition with a sclerotic rim, and internal calcification [5], [1]. The florid surrounding bone marrow edema on MRI in a young patient before skeletal maturity is highly characteristic [5], [1].
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Giant cell tumor, however this typically occurs in patients with closed growth plates and classically lacks a sclerotic margin or internal matrix calcifications [5], [1]. Additionally, giant cell tumors typically do not exhibit the extensive perilesional marrow edema seen in chondroblastoma [5].
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH), given its ability to present as a lucent lesion with surrounding edema in the pediatric population [5], [8]. Missing LCH could delay appropriate systemic workup, though LCH is typically diaphyseal or metaphyseal and usually lacks the punctate chondroid calcifications seen here [5].

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to characterise the lesion's margins, assess for any transphyseal or transcortical extension, and rule out a joint effusion [2].
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to determine the growth rate of the lesion and identify any subtle internal matrix calcifications that may have developed over time [9].
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend an MRI of the affected region without and with contrast, as this modality is superior here for defining the extent of the characteristic florid bone marrow and soft tissue edema, evaluating transphyseal extension, and identifying any secondary aneurysmal bone cyst components [4], [7], [2].
management.mriSequences: I would specify fluid-sensitive sequences such as STIR or T2 with fat saturation to optimally demonstrate the profound perilesional marrow edema, and T1 post-contrast imaging to assess the pattern of enhancement, which is typically moderate and heterogeneous [4], [2].
management.nuclearMedicine: A technetium-99m bone scan could be considered if there is diagnostic uncertainty, as chondroblastomas typically demonstrate increased radiotracer uptake [1].
management.intervention: If tissue diagnosis needed, I would specify an image-guided bone biopsy, taking care to target the solid non-cystic component if secondary aneurysmal bone cyst features are present [10].
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Sarcoma / Bone Tumour MDT [4]. My report would clearly document the exact location of the lesion, the extent of perilesional edema, and the integrity of the articular surface and growth plate to guide surgical curettage and bone grafting [2], [8].

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a well-defined, lucent lesion located within an epiphyseal equivalent, such as the patella, greater trochanter, or greater tuberosity [1], [2]. The lesion typically demonstrates a thin sclerotic margin and a narrow zone of transition [1], [3]. Supporting features include the presence of internal chondroid matrix calcifications, which are seen in approximately 50% of cases [1], [2]. On MRI, the lesion classically exhibits significant surrounding bone marrow and soft tissue edema, which is out of proportion to the size of the lesion [4], [1], [2]. Importantly, there is no evidence of a permeative or moth-eaten pattern of bone destruction to suggest an aggressive malignancy. Furthermore, there is no non-sclerotic margin or absence of internal matrix to suggest a giant cell tumor [5], [6]. The feature that most influences my thinking is the combination of a well-defined epiphyseal lucent lesion with a sclerotic margin and striking perilesional marrow edema in a skeletally immature patient [5], [7], [1].

The appearances are most in keeping with Chondroblastoma, given the epiphyseal equivalent location, narrow zone of transition with a sclerotic rim, and internal calcification [5], [1]. The florid surrounding bone marrow edema on MRI in a young patient before skeletal maturity is highly characteristic [5], [1]. The principle differential would be Giant cell tumor, however this typically occurs in patients with closed growth plates and classically lacks a sclerotic margin or internal matrix calcifications [5], [1]. Additionally, giant cell tumors typically do not exhibit the extensive perilesional marrow edema seen in chondroblastoma [5]. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH), given its ability to present as a lucent lesion with surrounding edema in the pediatric population [5], [8]. Missing LCH could delay appropriate systemic workup, though LCH is typically diaphyseal or metaphyseal and usually lacks the punctate chondroid calcifications seen here [5].

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to characterise the lesion's margins, assess for any transphyseal or transcortical extension, and rule out a joint effusion [2]. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to determine the growth rate of the lesion and identify any subtle internal matrix calcifications that may have developed over time [9]. I would recommend an MRI of the affected region without and with contrast, as this modality is superior here for defining the extent of the characteristic florid bone marrow and soft tissue edema, evaluating transphyseal extension, and identifying any secondary aneurysmal bone cyst components [4], [7], [2]. I would specify fluid-sensitive sequences such as STIR or T2 with fat saturation to optimally demonstrate the profound perilesional marrow edema, and T1 post-contrast imaging to assess the pattern of enhancement, which is typically moderate and heterogeneous [4], [2]. A technetium-99m bone scan could be considered if there is diagnostic uncertainty, as chondroblastomas typically demonstrate increased radiotracer uptake [1]. If tissue diagnosis needed, I would specify an image-guided bone biopsy, taking care to target the solid non-cystic component if secondary aneurysmal bone cyst features are present [10]. This should go to the Sarcoma / Bone Tumour MDT [4]. My report would clearly document the exact location of the lesion, the extent of perilesional edema, and the integrity of the articular surface and growth plate to guide surgical curettage and bone grafting [2], [8].
